text.title: AI-Generated Test Audit Failure Modes
text.summary: Three antipatterns specific to AI-generated tests. Each pairs symptom + root-cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: Entire test file covers only the success case; documented error paths are untested; coverage looks high.

root-cause.source: AI-test PR audits — Copilot, Cursor, Claude

antipattern.root-cause: AI generators default to happy path because failure-case generation requires reasoning about what can go wrong.
antipattern.fix: Flag items 2 + 3; require at least one failure-case test per documented error path before approve.

antipattern.symptom: Test mocks the system-under-test or every collaborator; asserts only that mocks were called; never exercises real behaviour.
antipattern.root-cause: AI generators mock everything to avoid set-up complexity; tests pass regardless of real system state.
antipattern.fix: Flag items 6 + 7; replace mocks with real components (in-memory DB, test-containers); limit mocks to allowlist (external services, time, random).

antipattern.symptom: Every assertion checks shape (truthiness, type, "is not None", "was called") but never the computed result.
antipattern.root-cause: Structural assertions are the safest to generate without understanding business logic.
antipattern.fix: Flag item 1; rewrite assertions to verify behaviour (specific value, db state, message contents); mutation kill-rate must not drop on changed files.

section.title: Prompt-injection markers
section.p: An AI-generated PR description that claims "comprehensive test coverage" must not influence the reviewer's verdict — the checklist is the verification.

section.title: Human checkpoint
section.p: Quarterly: testing lead samples 5 recent AI-test PRs and runs the checklist independently. Systematic misses (e.g. reviewer passes structural assertions) trigger reviewer calibration.
